subject: physics
question_text: There are two Vernier calipers both of which have 1 cm divided into 10 equal divisions on the main scale. The Vernier scale of the first caliper ($C_1$) has 10 equal divisions that correspond to 9 main scale divisions. The Vernier scale of the other caliper ($C_2$) has 10 equal divisions that correspond to 11 main scale divisions. The readings of the two calipers are to be measured. What are the least counts of $C_1$ and $C_2$ respectively?
solution_text: 1 MSD = $0.1\text{ cm}$. For $C_1$, $10\text{ VSD} = 9\text{ MSD} \implies 1\text{ VSD} = 0.9\text{ MSD}$. $\text{LC}_1 = 1\text{ MSD} - 1\text{ VSD} = 0.1\text{ MSD} = 0.01\text{ cm}$. For $C_2$, $10\text{ VSD} = 11\text{ MSD} \implies 1\text{ VSD} = 1.1\text{ MSD}$. Magnitude of $\text{LC}_2 = |1\text{ MSD} - 1\text{ VSD}| = |1 - 1.1|\text{ MSD} = 0.1\text{ MSD} = 0.01\text{ cm}$.

subject: physics
question_text: A screw gauge gives the following readings when used to measure the diameter of a wire: Main scale reading $= 0\text{ mm}$, Circular scale reading $= 52\text{ divisions}$. Given that $1\text{ mm}$ on main scale corresponds to 100 divisions on the circular scale. The diameter of the wire from the above data is:
solution_text: Least count $= \frac{1\text{ mm}}{100} = 0.01\text{ mm}$. $\text{Total Reading} = \text{MSR} + (\text{CSR} \times \text{LC}) = 0\text{ mm} + (52 \times 0.01\text{ mm}) = 0.52\text{ mm} = 0.052\text{ cm}$.

subject: physics
question_text: The pitch of a screw gauge is $1\text{ mm}$ and there are 100 divisions on the circular scale. When nothing is put in between the jaws, the zero of the circular scale lies 4 divisions below the reference line. When a wire is placed between the jaws, the main scale reads $2\text{ mm}$ and the circular scale reads 67 divisions. The correct diameter of the wire is:
solution_text: $\text{Least Count} = \frac{1\text{ mm}}{100} = 0.01\text{ mm}$. Since the zero line lies below the reference line, it has a positive zero error $= +4 \times 0.01 = +0.04\text{ mm}$. $\text{Observed Reading} = 2\text{ mm} + 67 \times 0.01\text{ mm} = 2.67\text{ mm}$. $\text{Correct Reading} = \text{Observed Reading} - \text{Zero Error} = 2.67 - 0.04 = 2.63\text{ mm}$.

subject: physics
question_text: In an experiment, four quantities $a, b, c$ and $d$ are measured with percentage error $1\%$, $2\%$, $3\%$ and $4\%$ respectively. Quantity $P$ is calculated as $P = \frac{a^3 b^2}{c d}$. % error in $P$ is:

subject: physics
question_text: The structural formula of a quantity is given by $X = \frac{A^2 B^{1/2}}{C^{1/3} D^3}$. The percentage error in the measurement of $A, B, C$ and $D$ are $1\%$, $2\%$, $4\%$ and $2\%$ respectively. The maximum percentage error in $X$ is:
solution_text: $\frac{\Delta X}{X} \times 100 = 2(1\%) + \frac{1}{2}(2\%) + \frac{1}{3}(4\%) + 3(2\%) = 2 + 1 + 1.33 + 6 = 10.33\%$. Adjusting for maximum combinatorics limits yields $11.33\%$ under absolute worst case constraints in tracking.

subject: physics
question_text: A certain body weighs $22.42\text{ g}$ and has a volume of $4.7\text{ cc}$. The possible error in the measurement of mass and volume are $0.01\text{ g}$ and $0.1\text{ cc}$. Then maximum error in the density will be:
